facts.fact: A bridge table (junction/associative table) converts a many-to-many relationship into two one-to-many relationships, enabling foreign key constraints and query accuracy in relational databases

Index cardinality directly impacts query performance: high-cardinality columns (many unique values) offer high selectivity; low-cardinality columns (many repeated values) often result in ineffective indexes and full table scans

Surrogate keys (system-generated) vs natural keys (business-derived): the hybrid approach dominates modern practice—use a surrogate primary key for stability and performance, enforce business uniqueness with UNIQUE constraints on natural keys
SCD Type 2 preserves historical data by creating a new record when data changes while keeping the original record unchanged. Each record includes timestamp or version ranges to track validity periods
PostgreSQL UNLOGGED tables skip Write-Ahead Logging (WAL), yielding 3x performance improvement (168 vs 485 TPS), but lose crash safety—data is automatically truncated on crash
The expand-and-contract pattern for zero-downtime schema migrations reduces lock durations from minutes to <50ms (metadata-only operations), eliminating ~99% of migration-related downtime

Cardinality explosion (Cartesian product) occurs when joining tables without proper conditions: if table A has 1,000 rows and table B has 5,000 rows, an unrestricted join produces 5 million rows instead of expected result size

Composite primary key column ordering is critical: a key defined as (tenant_id, event_time) efficiently supports queries filtered by tenant_id, but provides no benefit to queries filtered only by event_time due to leftmost prefix optimization

At Shopify, introducing composite primary keys optimized around query patterns yielded 5-6x improvement in elapsed query time for common high-capacity queries, with gains correlating linearly to reduced MySQL buffer pool page reads

Bridge tables in dimensional modeling require careful handling of aggregation: without proper grain definition, a fact table row associated with multiple dimension values creates semi-additive facts where summing all lines produces duplicated totals much higher than reality
Fan-out in SQL occurs when a LEFT JOIN on a one-to-many relationship duplicates rows—one row on the left matches multiple rows on the right, causing double-counting in aggregations

Stripe built DocDB, a petabyte-scale database on MongoDB with a three-tier architecture (Database Proxy, Chunk Metadata Service, Database Shards with replicas) to handle financial transaction durability and zero-downtime resharding
PostgreSQL query optimizer uses cardinality estimations to choose the most efficient execution plan; accurate statistics (updated regularly) are essential for the optimizer to avoid full table scans

Natural keys face the risk of cascading updates across multiple tables if the business attribute used as the key changes; composite natural keys compound this by adding complexity to joins and increasing index sizes

Shopify's primary database grew to terabytes; further table splitting would require cross-database transactions. The company adopted Vitess (YouTube's open-source sharding solution) and reorganized into logical Pods to scale horizontally

Temp tables in PostgreSQL are session-scoped and automatically dropped when the session ends; UNLOGGED tables persist across sessions but are truncated on crash, making them suitable for ETL staging and caching

Domain-driven design entities differ from relational database entities: DDD entities define boundaries by identity and aggregate roots, enforcing consistency, while relational entities represent every relationship via foreign keys

MySQL 8.0 supports INSTANT and INPLACE DDL operations; for full-table rebuilds, tools like gh-ost and pt-online-schema-change create shadow tables and apply changes in the background with zero downtime

examples.description: Employees table (emp_id, name) + Jobs table (job_id, title) resolved via bridge table Employee_Jobs (emp_id, job_id). Without the bridge, you cannot enforce constraints or query accurately

Shopify groups multiple shops into logical Pods, each containing multiple shops sharing the same MySQL database with a shop_id column for sharding. They migrated from monolithic schema to Vitess for horizontal scaling

Stripe's DocDB stores petabytes of financial transaction data across distributed shards with replicas. Key objects: customers, charges, invoices, subscriptions, balance_transactions, payment_methods. Every shard is replicated for durability
Netflix's data warehouse includes two fact tables (Sessions, Payments) and four dimensions (Date, Users, Shows, Plans) modeled on MySQL. Grain is individual sessions at transaction level
Emergency room admittance records have multiple initial diagnoses. Without a bridge table, you cannot store an admittance with multiple diagnoses without duplicating the admittance row or losing data

analogies.explanation: A many-to-many relationship (Songs ↔ Playlists) is like asking: 'Which songs are on which playlists?' Without a bridge table, you'd have to duplicate either the song or playlist record. The bridge table Playlist_Songs holds references to both—like a menu that lists the songs in each playlist

analogies.analogy: Composite primary key column order as reading order
analogies.explanation: A composite key (tenant_id, event_time) is like reading left-to-right: you can quickly find 'all events for tenant 1 in any time', but searching 'all events on March 5th across all tenants' requires a full scan, because you're starting from the right-hand column

Joining without proper conditions is like multiplying: 1000 rows × 5000 rows = 5M results. It's not that the join is wrong—it's that you've asked the wrong question. The database answers correctly, but the scale explodes

analogies.analogy: SCD Type 2 as a versioned record
analogies.explanation: When a customer address changes, SCD Type 2 is like keeping old business cards: you preserve the old address record (valid_from=2024-01-01, valid_to=2024-05-01) and create a new record for the current address. History stays intact

analogies.analogy: Surrogate keys as universal ID cards
analogies.explanation: A surrogate key (auto-increment ID) is like a government-issued ID number: immutable, unique, and independent of any attributes that might change (unlike using a Social Security number, which can be spoofed or belong to the wrong person)

key_numbers.value: 3x faster (485 TPS vs 168 TPS for regular tables)
key_numbers.context: Write-intensive workloads where durability is not critical (staging, caching, ETL)
Metadata-only operations reduce downtime by ~99% vs full table rewrites
Queries that consumed the most database capacity showed linear improvement in buffer pool page reads
Demonstrates how innocent-looking joins can produce results orders of magnitude larger than expected

key_numbers.value: High cardinality ≥ high selectivity; low cardinality ≤ low selectivity
key_numbers.context: Column uniqueness directly predicts index effectiveness

key_numbers.value: Leftmost prefix optimization (first N columns of composite key)
key_numbers.context: A 3-column key optimizes queries using 1 or 2 leading columns, but not trailing columns in isolation
Built on MongoDB with 3-tier architecture (Proxy, Metadata, Shards) for 24/7 uptime

common_misconceptions.misconception: A bridge table should have a surrogate primary key instead of a composite key
common_misconceptions.reality: Bridge tables are best designed with a composite primary key of the two foreign keys (e.g., PRIMARY KEY (employee_id, job_id)). This enforces uniqueness at the business level and simplifies queries. A surrogate key adds no value and obscures the intent

common_misconceptions.misconception: Natural keys should never be used because they can change
common_misconceptions.reality: Natural keys are valuable for business rule enforcement. Use a hybrid: surrogate PK for joins, UNIQUE constraint on natural keys for business rule validation. This gives you both performance and data integrity

common_misconceptions.misconception: Cardinality explosion is a query bug that the database should fix
common_misconceptions.reality: Cardinality explosion is a correct result for an ambiguous query. The database joins correctly; the problem is missing or wrong join conditions. The query must explicitly specify the join grain

common_misconceptions.misconception: SCD Type 2 is only for data warehouses, not operational databases
common_misconceptions.reality: SCD Type 2 patterns (versioned records with valid_from/valid_to timestamps) are useful in any system that needs audit trails or historical snapshots—audit logs, compliance systems, time-travel queries

common_misconceptions.misconception: A larger composite key hurts performance more than a surrogate key
common_misconceptions.reality: A 3-4 column composite key is typically more efficient than a separate surrogate if queries filter on those columns. The size increase is minimal; the selectivity gain is substantial

common_misconceptions.misconception: Temporary tables in PostgreSQL persist across sessions
common_misconceptions.reality: Temporary tables are session-scoped and auto-dropped when the session ends. UNLOGGED tables persist across sessions but are truncated on crash. These are different tools for different jobs

common_misconceptions.misconception: Schema migrations always require application downtime
common_misconceptions.reality: Expand-and-contract pattern with metadata-only operations enables zero-downtime migrations. The application can read/write both old and new schemas during the transition

common_misconceptions.misconception: Domain-driven design entities map directly to database tables
common_misconceptions.reality: DDD entities define boundaries by identity and aggregate roots (enforcing invariants); relational entities map every relationship via foreign keys. The impedance mismatch means DDD aggregates often span multiple tables
